channel.title: CeruleanOak — From the workshop
channel.description: Devlog posts from CeruleanOak — one developer, small games.

item.title: v0.4 — Hut Interiors, Dungeons, Full Audio, and a Major AI Overhaul
item.description: The biggest Pure Rogue update yet: enemy movement archetypes, real hut interiors, proper dungeon floors, zone-aware spawning, and full layered audio.
item.content:encoded:
This is the biggest update Pure Rogue has had.

Huts now have real interiors. Walk through the door and an entirely separate room renders in an overlay — with its own physics, combat, enemies, chests, and objects. Everything inside is independent from the exterior. Exit by walking south past the boundary.

Enemies have movement personalities. Five archetypes: Chaser (direct pursuit), Keeper (holds range, punishes rush-ins), Kiter (circle-strafes and times attack rushes), Jumper (discrete zigzag lunges), Ambusher (stationary until triggered, then an explosive burst). Each zone has a personality profile that draws from the archetypes that fit its character.

Zone-aware spawning is in. Green, Red, Cyan, Yellow, and Gray each have tiered enemy tables that scale with depth. No more generic bleed between zones.

Audio is fully layered now. Dynamic music with layers that respond to game state, plus SFX for aggro, blade attacks, bow charge, dodge roll, item destruction, and player death.

Play it here.

item.title: v0.3, a First Boss, and Two Instruments
item.description: Dynamic music is in, the first boss is coded, and the sound design constraint — square wave and noise only — is turning out to be the right call.
item.content:encoded:
March was a big month for Pure Rogue. v0.3 shipped with dynamic music, unlockable characters, themed zones, discoverable secrets, a reworked crafting menu, and a balance pass. Then I coded my first boss. It felt great.

The sound design constraint: two instruments only. Square wave and noise. Square wave has no dynamics, so everything comes from duration and pitch. The only way to add complexity is to layer — the green zone music is three discordant low square waves stacked on each other. Guttural. Never overstays its welcome. Noise is technically cheating but it's what makes weapon SFX feel physical, so I'm allowing it.

item.title: Got Distracted by DiceWars. Also Shipped Pure Rogue.
item.description: Fixed an old web game I've wasted too many hours on. Then shipped Pure Rogue v0.2 in the same month. Turns out distraction and productivity aren't mutually exclusive.
item.content:encoded:
I've been playing DiceWars since at least 2008 — territory control, dice mechanics, Risk without the three-hour commitment. It's open source, and there are things about it that have always bothered me. In February I finally fixed them: stripped the animations, reworked the pacing, removed the audio. It lives here.

Then I formally published Pure Rogue v0.2. Real-time combat, procedural rooms, crafting with hidden recipes, the dual REST/EXPLORE loop. It's a game now. Play it here.

item.title: Closing In on Full Migration
item.description: Collisions done, camera dialed in, saving and loading restored. Platforms and enemies are the last pieces — which happen to be the most interesting ones.
item.content:encoded: Collisions finalized. Camera dialed in. Saving and loading working with a proper player-facing menu. What's left is platforms and enemies — which are the game's entire variety beyond room layouts. The most interesting part, deferred until the architecture was solid enough to hold it.

item.title: The C++ Engine Runs Crisp (The Player Is Technically Present)
item.description: The GameMaker bugs are gone. The player is invisible — no sprites yet — but the collisions work beautifully and the framerate is what it should be. Hallelujah.
item.content:encoded: The bugs that used to haunt me in GameMaker are gone. The engine is running, collisions work, and the weird persistent glitches have stopped — because I own every layer now. The player is technically invisible (no sprites yet) but the behavior is correct, nearest-neighbor scaling is fixed, and the render order is properly 3D. It was a hallelujah moment.

item.title: Hanafuda Ships. GameMaker Gets a Farewell.
item.description: Shipped a full Hanafuda rebuild with two new game modes. Then started a C++ engine from scratch. Totally normal November.
item.content:encoded: Hanafuda shipped with a full visual rebuild, two new game modes (Sakura 2–4 player, Koi Koi Shop), and custom card back support. Then I set up C++ with SDL2 and OpenGL, watched the terminal print PENUMBRA initialized successfully, and said goodbye to GameMaker. The new setup does nothing yet. That's exactly where real things begin.

item.title: The Jury Is In on GameMaker
item.description: GameMaker is great for experiments. It is not great when the project outgrows what it was designed for. The deliberations were brief.
item.content:encoded: GameMaker is a good tool — for games that fit its assumptions. Penumbra has outgrown them. The camera system hit 3,000 lines. The platform resists what I'm trying to do. The verdict: C++. Direct memory control, real performance headroom, full GLSL shader access. The training wheels have to come off.

item.title: I Built My Own WaniKani Instead of Working on the Game
item.description: SMTP-powered adaptive flashcards, delivered by my home server. Stage two of the off-grid plan is underway. The game is also still happening.
item.content:encoded: Instead of working on the game I built a WaniKani replacement: SMTP-powered scheduled emails, adaptive quiz intervals, progress reports — all running on my home server. Stage two of moving my personal infrastructure (language learning, habits, exercise, diet) entirely off cloud services. It's a lot of work for something I could pay $10/month for. I find it satisfying for the same reason I find game dev satisfying.

item.title: Fifteen Years of Not Being a Programmer, Then This
item.description: Two years ago I wasn't a programmer. Now I'm building repos, shipping games, configuring APIs, and running a home server. Taking a moment to acknowledge what actually changed.
item.content:encoded:
Two years ago I was not a programmer. I'd been designing games my whole life but always relied on other people's tools. I told myself I wasn't a programmer person. I pussyfooted around it for fifteen years.

Now I'm building repos, publishing games, configuring MCP servers and APIs, managing a project backlog, running a home server. I want to be direct about what made the difference: generative AI. Not as a shortcut — as the thing that finally made the gap between "I know what I want to build" and "I know how to build it" crossable. Three game prototypes, two jam projects, a pile of experiments. I've become, in a real and functional sense, a programmer.

12-year-old me wanted to make his own Chrono Trigger. I have a full-time job and raise three kids. It's crazy. And it's happening.

item.title: Enemies Exist. Also: Clown Fiesta.
item.description: The enemy system is built. A stress test with way too many objects confirmed the performance is fine and the render order is out of control. Both expected outcomes.
item.content:encoded: Enemies exist. They can push each other, damage the player, bounce in arcs, travel in paths. The stress test — more objects than I'd ever actually use — confirmed performance is solid and render order is a clown fiesta. Platforms bouncing everything, coins getting pushed across the floor, enemies pile-upping in unlikely confluences. The render order problem is inherent to isometric and I've been putting it off. I have an idea for an elegant solution. It is definitely crazy, which means I have to try it.

item.title: Why Render Order Is the Hardest Problem in an Isometric Game
item.description: Overhauled the rendering pipeline so objects always draw in the correct depth order. Also: coins spill when you take damage. The prototype is starting to look like something a human could play.
item.content:encoded:
Isometric is 2D. The sense of depth is entirely an illusion maintained by draw order. This month I overhauled how the game handles it — anytime an object moves, the engine recalculates draw order for everything affected. The player always renders on top of the floor. Elevated platforms render above lower ones. It's foundational and invisible, which is the bar for this kind of work.

Also new: coin spill mechanic. Take damage or fall too far and your coins scatter — retrievable, but at the cost of a moment of vulnerability.

item.title: Now I Have to Learn Aseprite
item.description: Tile selection in the editor, a landing/crash mechanic, half-height tile differences, and cleaned-up room transitions. The prototype is taking shape. Sprites are next.
item.content:encoded: Tile type selection is in the editor menu. Landing and crashing is in — fall too far and you'll pay for it. Half-height tile differences are supported, which adds visual interest and navigation variety without complicating the systems underneath. Room transitions are clean. The prototype is starting to feel like a space you actually want to explore. Next: sprites. Time to stop being jank and learn Aseprite.

item.title: The Part Where I Decided to Make My Own Chrono Trigger
item.description: Where this started. November 2023, GameMaker, a blank project, and a game concept that 12-year-old me would have drawn in the margins of a notebook.
item.content:encoded: November 2023. GameMaker. A blank project and an idea for a citadel full of puzzle rooms run by robots and a master AI. You rescue characters who become playable, each with different abilities. Losing them sends you back to clear rooms you'd already bypassed. First month: collision detection, room switching, enemy detection and stealth, fake 3D elevation, character swapping. The game is called Penumbra. Everything on this site is a side effect of sitting down and refusing to not make something.
